summary: Iranian-French graphic novelist and filmmaker. Best known for Persepolis, a semi-autobiographical graphic memoir about growing up during and after the 1979 Iranian Revolution. Also wrote the graphic novel Embroideries. The protagonist of Persepolis (Marji) is sent to Vienna at age 14, returns to Iran, attends art school, and eventually moves to France.

works.name: Persepolis
works.description: A semi-autobiographical graphic memoir about growing up during the 1979 Iranian Revolution. The protagonist Marji has nightly discussions with God as a child. She reads the Marxist comic 'Dialectical Materialism' and is mentored by her uncle Anoosh, who gives her bread swans before his execution. She rebels by listening to Michael Jackson and Kim Wilde. She is sent to boarding school in Vienna at age 14 after becoming politically outspoken. She returns to Iran and sees her friend Nami fall off a building after a wine-drinking party is shut down by religious police. She parodies Michelangelo's Pieta by drawing Mary holding a dead soldier to gain admission to art school. She designs an amusement park based on legendary characters for her college thesis. She divorces her husband Reza. She sits in a bathtub for hours after hearing about how her grandfather was tortured. God tells her in a dream that an exam will be about a Montesquieu essay on slavery. When a nun yells at her for eating pasta out of a pot, she replies 'You were all prostitutes before becoming nuns.' Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Marjane Satrapi is an Iranian-French graphic novelist best known for Persepolis, a semi-autobiographical graphic memoir about growing up during and after the 1979 Iranian Revolution. The protagonist Marji has nightly discussions with God as a child and reads the Marxist comic 'Dialectical Materialism.' She is mentored by her uncle Anoosh, who gives her bread swans before his execution, after which she loses faith in God. She sits in a bathtub for hours after hearing about how her grandfather was tortured. She rebels by listening to Michael Jackson and Kim Wilde. She becomes politically outspoken and is sent to boarding school in Vienna at age 14. While in Vienna, a nun yells at her for eating pasta out of a pot, and she replies 'You were all prostitutes before becoming nuns.' She returns to Iran and sees her friend Nami fall off a building after a wine-drinking party is shut down by religious police. She parodies Michelangelo's Pieta by drawing Mary holding a dead soldier to gain admission to art school. God tells her in a dream that an exam will be about a Montesquieu essay on slavery, and she gets the highest score. She designs an amusement park based on legendary characters for her college thesis, though the design is rejected. She divorces her husband Reza and eventually moves to France.

Satrapi also wrote Embroideries (2003), a graphic novel in which the protagonist recalls selling cigarettes to pay for an operation on her grandmother's nose and serves tea to six old women who discuss the title euphemism for vaginal surgery.

Persepolis is frequently clued alongside Reading Lolita in Tehran by Azar Nafisi, as both deal with life under the Iranian regime.
